doc_name: When the MDR fails its own test: why the EU Medical Device Regulation does not meet the standards it imposes - FEPPD

chunks.doc_name: When the MDR fails its own test: why the EU Medical Device Regulation does not meet the standards it imposes - FEPPD
chunks.text: ![FEPPD](https://feppd.eu/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/logo_FEPPD.jpg) ![FEPPD](https://feppd.eu/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/logo_FEPPD-retina.jpg) ![When the MDR fails its own test: why the EU Medical Device Regulation does not meet the standards it imposes](https://feppd.eu/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/non_compliant_kl.png "non_compliant_kl") ## When the MDR fails its own test: why the EU Medical Device Regulation does not meet the standards it imposes **The EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR, Regulation (EU) 2017/745) imposes exceptionally high requirements on manufacturers of medical devices. Transparency, traceability, risk-based proportionality, validation, governance and post-market surveillance are presented as essential conditions for patient safety and for the smooth functioning of the internal market. Dental laboratories across Europe have invested significant time, expertise and resources to comply with these obligations.** **A fundamental question is now being raised by academic research: what would happen if the MDR itself were assessed against the very standards it imposes on manufacturers?** **The MDR assessed against its own rules** This question is examined in the peer-reviewed scientific publication “Deficiencies of the EU Medical Device Regulation when applying its own rules”, authored by Martin Haimerl, Michael D’Agosto, Mike Fornefett, Massimo Kubon and Thomas Schiepp, published in Frontiers in Medicine in January 2026. The authors analyse the MDR as if it were a regulated product and reach a clear conclusion: if the MDR were subject to its own regulatory logic, it would not meet the requirements for placing devices on the market. 1) Using established principles from product development and good regulatory practice, the authors examine whether the MDR defines clear objectives, translates them into traceable requirements, provides validated tools and infrastructure, and applies systematic risk management and governance. These are precisely the principles that the MDR itself requires manufacturers to follow. The analysis shows that the regulation lacks clear and measurable objectives, does not ensure traceability between goals and implementation, and does not provide evidence that its own effectiveness has been verified or validated. Core infrastructure such as EUDAMED was not fully available, tested or validated when the MDR became applicable. In addition, the MDR does not contain a functioning system for risk management at the level of the regulatory framework itself, despite mandating continuous risk management for medical devices throughout their lifecycle. In short, the MDR demands a level of regulatory discipline from manufacturers that it does not consistently apply to itself. **Why this matters for dental laboratories** For the dental technology sector, especially for manufacturers of custom-made dental devices, these findings are not abstract. They reflect everyday regulatory experience in a sector that does not produce serially manufactured medical devices, but patient-specific, bespoke solutions. The MDR was largely designed around industrial manufacturing models. When this logic is applied without sufficient differentiation to custom-made dental devices, proportionality and legal certainty are put at risk. The academic critique, therefore, resonates strongly with the concerns repeatedly raised by FEPPD. **Oversight without sector reality: lessons from JAMS 2.0**In its analysis of the CAMD-Europe JAMS 2.0 joint inspection report, FEPPD highlighted a clear disconnect between regulatory oversight activities and the reality of dental laboratories. The joint inspections conducted under the Joint Action on Market Surveillance focused predominantly on medium and large manufacturers of serially produced, higher-risk devices.

chunks.doc_name: When the MDR fails its own test: why the EU Medical Device Regulation does not meet the standards it imposes - FEPPD
chunks.text: strongly with the concerns repeatedly raised by FEPPD. **Oversight without sector reality: lessons from JAMS 2.0**In its analysis of the CAMD-Europe JAMS 2.0 joint inspection report, FEPPD highlighted a clear disconnect between regulatory oversight activities and the reality of dental laboratories. The joint inspections conducted under the Joint Action on Market Surveillance focused predominantly on medium and large manufacturers of serially produced, higher-risk devices. Custom-made dental devices were not treated as a distinct category, and the specific characteristics and risk profile of dental laboratories were not evaluated. FEPPD emphasised that inspection approaches developed for industrial manufacturing cannot simply be transferred to bespoke dental technology without undermining proportionality and legal certainty. 2) This practical observation aligns directly with the academic finding that the MDR repeatedly refers to a risk-based approach, but does not implement it consistently in practice. Risk classification and regulatory obligations are often derived from generic categories rather than from the actual, demonstrated risk of individual devices or production models. **Regulatory clarity and the manufacturer debate**Another example of structural inconsistency is the long-standing debate on who qualifies as a manufacturer of custom-made devices under the MDR. In its publication “To be or pretend not to be a manufacturer of CMDs: FEPPD defends equal MDR compliance”, FEPPD addressed attempts to exclude chairside CAD/CAM production by dentists from manufacturer obligations. 3) This debate illustrates a broader governance issue also identified in the academic publication. When definitions are not applied clearly and consistently, enforcement becomes fragmented and unequal. Such ambiguity directly contradicts the MDR’s own principles of transparency, traceability and impartiality. **EUDAMED as a structural weakness** EUDAMED provides another striking example of the MDR failing to meet its own standards. Haimerl and his co-authors note that a medical device cannot be placed on the market without all essential accessories being available, validated and functional. Yet EUDAMED, which is central to the operation of the MDR, remained incomplete and unvalidated for years after the regulation entered into force. For dental laboratories, which do not place serial products on the market and do not use UDI systems, EUDAMED offers little or no additional safety value. Nevertheless, its delayed and uneven implementation has created administrative uncertainty and divergent national practices. This represents exactly the type of failed design transfer from regulatory intent to operational reality identified in the scientific analysis. **A regulation that demands more than it delivers** Taken together, the academic findings and FEPPD’s practical experience lead to the same conclusion. The problem is not regulation as such, but the quality, proportionality and governance of the regulatory system. Proportionality must be more than a recital. Risk-based approaches must be grounded in real risk rather than generic categories. Infrastructure must be available before obligations are enforced. Governance must be continuous, transparent and documented. Manufacturers are required to demonstrate compliance before placing a device on the market. According to the authors of the Frontiers in Medicine study, the regulatory system itself should be held to the same standard. Until the MDR is evaluated and corrected in line with its own principles, dental laboratories will continue to bear the

chunks.text: obligations are enforced. Governance must be continuous, transparent and documented. Manufacturers are required to demonstrate compliance before placing a device on the market. According to the authors of the Frontiers in Medicine study, the regulatory system itself should be held to the same standard. Until the MDR is evaluated and corrected in line with its own principles, dental laboratories will continue to bear the burden of a framework that demands excellence without consistently demonstrating it. ### Like this post? ### More posts ![AGM 2022 in Paris](https://feppd.eu/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/feppdagmgroupphoto-580x410.jpg "FEPPD AGM 2020 - copie") ###### [AGM 2022 in Paris](https://feppd.eu/agm-2022-in-paris/) ![AGM 2022 in Paris](https://feppd.eu/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/FEPPD-AGM-2020-copie-2-580x410.jpg "FEPPD AGM 2020 - copie") ###### [AGM 2022 in Paris](https://feppd.eu/agm-2022-paris/) ![Dental technicians & Medical Device Regulation](https://feppd.eu/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/dental-technicians-medical-device-regulation-580x410.jpg "dental-technicians-medical-device-regulation") ###### [Dental technicians & Medical Device Regulation](https://feppd.eu/dental-technicians-medical-device-regulation/) ### Leave a Reply [Cancel Reply](/when-mdr-fails-its-own-test-eu-medical-device-regulation/#respond) Your email address will not be published.
